attributes.stem:
A 20-year-old overseas visitor is brought to the Emergency Department in the early hours of the morning by one of her friends. The woman is complaining of abdominal pain and lethargy. They have been out most of the night and the two of them have consumed an estimated 15 standard drinks of alcohol, but deny any illicit substance use. The patient has vomited once about an hour ago consisting of normal food-containing bile. She has no other symptoms. Her last menstrual period was seven days ago and was regular. She has a regular cycle without any complications. On examination, her blood pressure is 112/70 mmHg, pulse 110/min, temperature 37.8°C, respiratory rate 13/min, and SpO2 100% on room air. She has widespread abdominal tenderness and guarding. There is no evidence of hepato-splenomegaly. She has normal bowel sounds.
An ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 110/min. Her laboratory studies show:

  • Full blood count:

    • WCC 5.2 x109/L (4.0-11.0)

    • Hb 78 g/L (116-150)

    • MCV 83 fL (80-100)

    • Neutrophils 6.8 x109/L (1.7-7.5)

  • Reticulocytes 13% (0.5-2.5)

  • UEC, LFTs, pancreatic enzymes, CRP all normal

  • B-HCG negative
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Sickle cell disease is a condition of red blood cells in which an inherited (autosomal recessive) mutation at the beta globin gene allele (substitution normal glutamic acid for valine) inhibits the formation of the normal architectural of the red blood cell. As a result, the red blood cell typically resembles a crescent moon shape, which has a reduced oxygen carrying capacity and have an increased ability to adhere to the vascular endothelium causing obstruction. When there is vascular occlusion, pain may elicited from downstream ischaemia of the tissue which in the case above is most likely occurring in the mesenteric vasculature. The episodes of pain may be triggered by alcohol (as in the case here), infection, cold climate, dehydration and stress.

Diagnosis is made by analysis of blood film which shows the characteristic crescent shaped red blood cells and the presence of Howell-Jolly bodies, as well as haemoglobin electrophoresis demonstrating HbS haemoglobin presence. Often the patient has a normocytic, normochromic anaemia. Note that sickle cell disease is more prevalent in African and equatorial populations of people, where is has a survival advantage for carriers against malaria. Hence, as the stem outlines this patient is a visitor from overseas and this is a potential clue to the origins of this condition.

Sickle cell crisis is the most likely diagnosis in this scenario. The patient presents with abdominal pain, lethargy, and a history of significant alcohol consumption, which can precipitate a vaso-occlusive crisis in individuals with sickle cell disease. These cells can obstruct blood vessels, causing a vaso-occlusive crisis, which is characterised by severe pain due to tissue ischaemia. The laboratory findings of normocytic anaemia with a high reticulocyte count suggest increased red blood cell turnover, consistent with sickle cell disease. Triggers for these crises include dehydration, infection, cold exposure, stress, and alcohol consumption, as seen in this case. Complications of vaso-occlusive crises can include acute chest syndrome, stroke, and organ damage due to repeated ischaemic episodes. The absence of other significant findings such as elevated inflammatory markers or pancreatic enzymes further supports this diagnosis.

This is less likely to be gastroenteritis. Whilst she is febrile and has a history of vomiting, often the the presenting symptoms include high volume of vomiting and/or diarrhoea (+/- haematochezia) plus abdominal pain. It does not often present so acutely with normocytic anaemia without the presence of passage of blood and if it were severe enough to do so, it would more likely to be a microcytic anaemia.

Acute pancreatitis is inflammation of the pancreas that results in severe, sudden-onset mid-epigastric to left upper quadrant abdominal pain, sometimes described as radiating to the back. Patients often present with associated nausea and vomiting. Gallstones and alcohol abuse are the most common causes of acute pancreatitis. Diagnosis is confirmed by raised pancreatic enzymes on blood tests (lipase and amylase), which have been outlines as normal above, making this diagnosis less likely.

Appendicitis does typically acutely present with generalised abdominal pain with nausea/vomiting an accompanying feature. This pain may migrate to the right lower quadrant as the parietal perineum becomes more inflamed. This is not often accompanied by signs of anaemia and is likely to have a raised CRP.

Ovarian torsion is a gynaecological emergency which refers to the complete rotation of the ovary around its ligamentous attachment which results in obstruction of the blood supply to the organ. This typically presents with pelvic pain, nausea and occasionally low grade fever. As the abdominal pain is more generalised and an anaemia present, this is less likely to be the case. Similarly, CRP is likely to be raised in this condition.
learningPoints: Acute painful episodes may occur with normocytic anaemia in patients with sickle cell disease (especially immigrants from areas where sickle cell disease is more prevalent).
Vaso-occlusive crises in sickle cell disease are triggered by factors such as dehydration, infection, and alcohol consumption.
references: Rees DC, Williams TN, Gladwin MT. Sickle-cell disease. Lancet. 2010 Dec 11;376(9757):2018-31. doi: 10.1016/S0140-6736(10)61029-X. Epub 2010 Dec 3. PMID: 21131035.
Bainbridge R, Higgs DR, Maude GH, Serjeant GR. Clinical presentation of homozygous sickle cell disease. J Pediatr. 1985 Jun;106(6):881-5. doi: 10.1016/s0022-3476(85)80230-4. PMID: 2582106.

Borhade MB, Patel P, Kondamudi NP. Sickle Cell Crisis. [Updated 2024 Feb 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK526064/
